ROGII — Spatial Geology EDA

Map-vs-geology view of the rogii-wellbore-geology-prediction dataset. Generated by report/generate_geological_map.py from 773 training wells × every 40-ft MD sample = 127,680 formation-top observations interpolated onto a 140×110 grid.

1. The Play in One Paragraph

The 773 training wells + 3 visible test wells cover a contiguous 36 × 27-mile block (≈ 960 mi² of lease area). The geological column visible in typewell labels matches the South Texas Eagle Ford trend: from shallow to deep, ANCC (Anacacho) → ASTNU/ASTNL (Austin Chalk Upper/Lower) → EGFDU/EGFDL (Eagle Ford Upper/Lower) → BUDA (Buda Limestone). Eagle Ford is the productive zone — the median Eagle Ford reservoir thickness (EGFDU → BUDA) is about 162 ft across the play, with the top of the Eagle Ford sitting at a median elevation of -9,038 ft (typical TVT ≈ 10,538 ft below surface, since Z is elevation, not depth-from-surface). Structural dip is gentle: median ≈ 3.96%, but the structure does tilt systematically across the play.

2. Headline Numbers

Play footprint
36.2 × 26.5 mi
Wells
773 train + 3 test
Sampled obs
127,680
Top of Eagle Ford (median)
-9,038 ft
Eagle Ford thickness (median)
162 ft
Eagle Ford thickness (p5–p95)
126 – 206 ft
Median structural dip
3.96%
p95 structural dip
84.09%

3. Structure Contour Maps — All Six Formations

For each of the six formation tops, we have an elevation observation at every sampled MD point of every training horizontal well. Linear interpolation onto a regular grid gives a structure-contour map per formation. Red = shallow, blue = deep. White stars are the three visible test wells.

All six surfaces share the same overall structural pattern — they are parallel beds dipping in roughly the same direction. This is the spatial manifestation of the “nearby wells' typewells are offset copies” insight from the EDA: same stratigraphy + small regional dip means every neighbour's typewell sees the same GR sequence with a depth shift dictated by the dip.

4. Isopach Maps — Where Each Layer Is Thick or Thin

Subtracting consecutive structure-contour surfaces gives the local layer thickness. Yellow = thin, red = thick. These are the conventional isopachs reservoir geologists use to plan landing zones.

The Eagle Ford intervals (EGFDU and EGFDL) are spatially the most variable — they thicken and thin meaningfully across the play. The Austin Chalk intervals above them are more uniform.

5. The Target Zone — Eagle Ford Reservoir

Almost every horizontal well in this dataset is drilled within the Eagle Ford (between the EGFDU top and the BUDA top). The left panel shows the depth of the Eagle Ford top — i.e. how deep each well had to drill before reaching the target. The right panel shows the Eagle Ford reservoir thickness — the vertical window the lateral can stay inside.

Insight for modelling. The test wells (white stars) fall in regions where: (a) the Eagle Ford top is at typical depth, and (b) Eagle Ford thickness is in the dataset's normal range. There is no extrapolation risk — they sit firmly in the training distribution. This is good news: training-set-derived inversion priors should transfer cleanly.

6. Structural Dip — Magnitude and Direction

Numerically differentiating the smoothed Eagle Ford top gives, at each grid cell, the local dip slope (% rise/fall per horizontal foot) and the down-dip direction. The colormap shows magnitude; the white arrows point down-dip.

The play has a coherent regional structural trend — arrows align across neighbouring cells, which means the “use nearest wells' geology as a prior” trick is sound. Where you see swirls or arrow chaos, the dip is very small (essentially flat zones) and the noisy interpolation dominates.

7. Cross-Sections Through the Play

Slicing the interpolated structure surfaces along the East–West and North–South center lines gives true cross-sections of the geology. Coloured bands are the formation intervals; lines are the formation tops.

Two things to read off these plots:

  • The beds are nearly parallel — all six surfaces dip in tandem. That makes the Tikhonov smoothness prior (penalize curvature of the TVT path) well-justified.
  • The Eagle Ford zone tilts a few hundred feet across the 30-mile block — meaningful for any well that crosses geographically through it, and exactly why predicting TVT is more than constant extrapolation.

8. Per-Well Statistics on the Map

Three quantities per well, painted at the well's XY centroid: (a) minimum TVT — the deepest part of the lateral, a proxy for landing depth; (b) mean horizontal GR — a coarse lithology indicator; (c) lateral azimuth in degrees — the compass direction the well was drilled.

Lateral azimuth (right panel) clearly clusters in two dominant directions — matches the EDA finding that the field has two principal drilling orientations, likely chosen perpendicular to the regional maximum-horizontal-stress direction for fracture-network efficiency.

9. What This Means for the Competition

  • One play, one geological column. Models can assume a single shared stratigraphy — no need to generalize across different basins or formations.
  • Test wells are inside the training distribution. No out-of-distribution surprise on the depth axis. Whatever prior you build from training will apply.
  • Regional dip is small and coherent. Linear-Z baseline succeeds precisely because dip is locally stable. But local variability — the swirls in the dip map — explains the long tail where naive baselines fail.
  • Offset-well priors are physically defensible. The structure contours show neighbouring wells really are sampling the same dipping surface; a stacked super-typewell is essentially a Monte Carlo estimate of the same regional structure.
  • Two drilling directions ⇒ two failure modes. A model that uses lateral azimuth as an input feature should help, especially for laterals that drilled across dip rather than along it.

This is a spatial complement to the EDA in eda_report.html (per-well statistics) and the formal problem framing in competition_overview.html. The consolidated knowledge-base index is index.html.
